hindiMeaning: सूर्य अष्टोत्तर शतनामावली भगवान सूर्य के 108 पवित्र नामों का संग्रह है। इन नामों में सूर्यदेव के विभिन्न रूपों और गुणों का वर्णन है - सूर्य (प्रकाशदाता), आदित्य (अदिति पुत्र), भास्कर (प्रकाशकर), रवि (सूर्य), सवित्र (सृष्टिकर्ता), मार्तण्ड (मृत अण्ड से उत्पन्न), ग्रहराज (ग्रहों के राजा), दिनकर (दिन का कर्ता), सूर्यनारायण (सूर्य रूपी नारायण) आदि।
benefits: Reciting 108 names of Surya bestows health, vitality, and radiance
Traditionally recited for removal of eye diseases and grants strong vision
Used during Surya puja, Chhath, and Makar Sankranti archana
Nullifies malefic effects of Sun in horoscope
Particularly powerful on Sundays, Makar Sankranti, and Chhath Puja
description: The Surya Ashtottara Shatanamavali, known in Sanskrit as सूर्य अष्टोत्तर शतनामावली, is a devotional recitation of 108 sacred names in praise of Sun God Surya. Rooted in Surya Purana / Aditya Hridayam tradition, this composition carries deep spiritual significance. Devotees recite this prayer to used during Surya puja, Chhath, and Makar Sankranti archana and remove eye diseases and grant strong vision. It is also said to nullify malefic effects of Sun in horoscope. Recitation is particularly auspicious on Sunday (Ravivar) and during Makar Sankranti, Chhath Puja, and Ratha Saptami. At 108 verses long, the recitation takes roughly 15 minutes, making it accessible for regular devotional use. On this page, you can read the complete Surya Ashtottara Shatanamavali in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Surya Ashtottara Shatanamavali?
seoFaqs.answer: The Surya Ashtottara Shatanamavali (सूर्य अष्टोत्तर शतनामावली) is a devotional recitation of 108 sacred names of Lord Surya — the Sun God who is the visible Vedic deity (pratyaksha-devata) and the source of all life, light, and time on earth. Each name is recited with the prefix Om and suffix Namah (Om Suryaya Namah, Om Adityaya Namah, Om Bhaskaraya Namah and so on) — making the namavali ideal for archana (flower-offering) puja during Surya Sandhya, Ratha Saptami, Chhath Puja, and Makar Sankranti. The 108 names cover every aspect of the Sun: His twelve Aditya forms (Dvadasha-Aditya), His role as Graha-raja (king of planets), His Kashyapa-Aditi parentage, His sons Yama-Shani-Karna-Manu-Sugriva, and His ultimate identity as Surya-Narayana. The composition is preserved in classical Surya-upasana tradition. (Source: Surya Purana / Aditya Hridayam tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: How is Ashtottara Shatanamavali different from Sahasranama and Ashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: All three are name-based stotras but differ in length and ritual use. (1) Ashtottara Shatanamavali (108-name namavali) is recited in the namavali format — Om [Name] Namah — and is the standard chant for archana puja where 108 flowers, tulsi-leaves, kumkum or akshata are offered one per name. (2) Sahasranama (1000 names) is recited as continuous shlokas in anushtubh metre and takes 30-45 minutes — used for serious uddhata-sankalpa worship like Vishnu Sahasranama or Lalita Sahasranama. (3) Ashtakam (8 verses) is a compact stuti in classical metres — used for daily japa rather than archana. The Surya Ashtottara is therefore the everyday devotional namavali for Sun-worship that fits comfortably into 15 minutes of morning Surya-puja. (Sources: classical archana paddhati; namavali tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: When should the Surya Ashtottara Shatanamavali be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The Surya Ashtottara is most powerfully recited at sunrise on Sunday (Ravivar — Surya's day), Ratha Saptami (Magha Shukla Saptami — celebrated as Surya Jayanti), Makar Sankranti (when the Sun enters Capricorn — January 14), all four days of Chhath Puja (Kartik Shukla Chaturthi to Saptami), Samba Dashami in Odisha (Pausha Krishna Ashtami — birthday of Surya's son Samba), Aditya Hridaya parayana days, and during the Pitru Paksha for Sun-worship. Astrologically, it is prescribed for those with weak Sun (Surya) in the natal chart, during Surya Mahadasha or Antardasha, for Sade Sati relief (since Shani is Surya's son), for eyesight problems, skin diseases, low confidence, father-related issues, and government job aspirations. The chant should be done facing east, ideally after Surya Arghya with a copper kalash. (Sources: Surya upasana paddhati; Vedic astrology remedy tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the spiritual benefits of chanting 108 names of Surya?
seoFaqs.answer: Reciting the Surya Ashtottara is held to bestow: (1) physical health and vitality — Surya is the deity of arogya (wellness), particularly cure of eye diseases (since the Sun is Loka-chakshu — eye of the world), skin diseases (Kushtha-roga), heart ailments, and bone strength; (2) confidence, leadership, and royal-status (since Surya is Graha-raja); (3) success in government, public office, and authority positions; (4) good father-relationship and progeny (since Surya is Pita — the cosmic father); (5) graha-shanti — pacification of malefic Sun in the horoscope and reduction of Shani Sade Sati severity (because Shani is Surya's son); (6) brilliance of mind, fame, and tejas (radiance); (7) ultimately moksha through the Surya-mandala marg of Upanishadic liberation. The phala-shruti is preserved in classical tradition. (Sources: Aditya Hridayam phala-shruti; Brihat Parashara Hora Shastra Surya remedies.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the significance of names like Martanda, Vivasvan, Pushan and Hiranyagarbha?
seoFaqs.answer: Each of the 108 names reveals a unique aspect of Surya: (1) Martanda (मार्तण्ड) — born from a dead-egg, referring to the Vedic story where Aditi's eighth son was born as a lifeless mass that Indra reshaped into the Sun; (2) Vivasvan (विवस्वान्) — the brilliant one, the Vedic name of Surya as father of Manu Vaivasvata (progenitor of the current human race) and Yama-Yami; (3) Pushan (पूषन्) — nourisher and protector of cattle and travellers, one of the Twelve Adityas; (4) Hiranyagarbha (हिरण्यगर्भ) — golden-womb, the cosmic seed from which the universe sprouted (Rigveda 10.121); (5) Bhaskara — light-maker; (6) Aditya — son of Aditi; (7) Savitr — impeller (deity of the Gayatri Mantra); (8) Ravi — the swift; (9) Surya-Narayana — Surya as a form of Narayana-Vishnu; (10) Tamoghna — destroyer of darkness/ignorance. Together they cover Surya as physical sun, life-force, time-keeper, planetary lord, and supreme Brahman. (Sources: Rigveda 1.50; Yajurveda Aruna prashna; Markandeya Purana Sapta-shati prologue.)

seoFaqs.question: Can the Surya Ashtottara be recited during Chhath Puja and Makar Sankranti?
seoFaqs.answer: Absolutely — these are the two most powerful festival contexts for the Surya Ashtottara recitation. During Chhath Puja (the four-day Kartik Surya festival of Bihar, Jharkhand, Eastern UP, and Nepal) the namavali is chanted at the ghat while offering arghya to the setting sun on the third day (Sandhya Arghya) and the rising sun on the fourth day (Usha Arghya) — each of the 108 names accompanied by an offering of til, gud, fruits, or thekua to Chhathi Maiya and Surya Narayana. During Makar Sankranti (when the Sun enters Capricorn signaling Uttarayana) the namavali is recited at sunrise after sacred bathing in Ganga, Yamuna, or Sangam — accompanied by til-gud daan and copper-vessel arghya. Ratha Saptami (Magha Shukla Saptami) is also a major namavali recitation day across South India, especially at Tirumala where the Surya-prabha vahana procession of Lord Venkateshwara takes place. (Sources: Drik Panchang festival calendar; Chhath Puja paddhati.)
